docs.title: Welcome to MkDocs
docs.text: For full documentation visit mkdocs.org.

docs.title: Commands
docs.text:
• mkdocs new [dir-name] - Create a new project.
  • mkdocs serve - Start the live-reloading docs server.
  • mkdocs build - Build the documentation site.
  • mkdocs -h - Print help message and exit.

docs.title: Project layout
docs.text:
mkdocs.yml    # The configuration file.
docs/
    index.md  # The documentation homepage.
    ...       # Other markdown pages, images and other files.

docs.title: HTTP request methods
docs.text: Method Description GET Retrieve data (should not modify data) POST Create a new resource PUT Modify/Update an existing resource PATCH Modify part of an existing resource DELETE Delete an existing resources

docs.title: Plural nouns
docs.text:
It helps ensure consistency and better reflects the possibility of the endpoint returning multiple resources

⛔ GET /category ✅ GET /categories

⛔ GET /user ✅ GET /users

docs.title: Separate words with hyphens
docs.text:
Use hyphens - to improve the readability of URIs, do not use underscores _

⛔ GET /managed_devices ✅ GET /managed-devices

⛔ GET /myFolders ✅ GET /my-folders

docs.title: Use lowercase letters
docs.text:
Lowercase letters should be consistently preferred in URI paths

⛔ GET /Categories ✅ GET /categories

⛔ GET /USERS ✅ GET /users

docs.title: Use path variables for singleton resource
docs.text:
GET /categories

will return a collection of resource categories

GET /categories/{id}

will return a singleton resource a category

docs.title: Use query param to filter a collection
docs.text:
⛔ GET /categories/search-by-name/{name} ✅ GET /categories?name={name}

⛔ GET /users/username/{username} ✅ GET /users?username={username}

docs.title: Sub resources
docs.text:
GET /categories/{id}/posts

will return the list of posts per a category

GET /posts/{id}/categories

will return the list of categories per a post

docs.title: Version your endpoints
docs.text:
Helps to easily manage changes and updates to an API while still maintaining backward compatibility

GET /v1/categories

GET /v2/categories

docs.title: Do not use verbs in the URI
docs.text:
HTTP methods (GET, POST, PUT, DELETE, etc.) are used to perform actions on those resources, effectively acting as verbs

⛔ POST /v1/categories/create ✅ POST /v1/categories

⛔ POST /v1/categories/update ✅ PUT /v1/categories

docs.title: Use Cases
docs.text: Endpoint Use Case GET /v1/categories Get all categories GET /v1/categories/{id} Get category by id GET /v1/categories/{id}/posts Get all post of a certain categories GET /v1/posts?date=20-01-2024 Search posts by created date POST /v1/categories Create a new category PUT /v1/categories/{id} Update an existing category PATCH /v1/categories/{id} Update a sub property of an existing category DELETE /v1/categories/{id} Delete an existing category by id

docs.title: HTTP Status Code
docs.text:
HTTP defines these standard status codes that can be used to convey the results of a client’s requests. They are divided into five categories

Status Description 1xx Informational 2xx Success 3xx Redirection 4xx Client error 5xx Server error

Here are the most common HTTP status codes

Status Description 200 Ok Request was successful 201 Created Request was successful and a new resource was created 400 Bad request Server couldn’t process the request to a client error 500 Internal server error Server encountered an unexpected exception 403 Forbidden User don’t have the permissions to access the resource 404 Not found Resource not found 503 Service unavailable Server not able to handle requests

docs.title: Text processing in command line with awk
docs.text: awk is a command-line tool used for pattern scanning and processing. It's helps search, extract and transform text based on patterns (like regular expressions).

docs.title: 05 - Text processing with awk
docs.text:
By default, awk treats whitespace (spaces or tabs) as the field separator. However, if you're working with CSV or tab-delimited files, you can easily change the field separator with the -F flag:

awk -F ',' '{ print $1, $3 }' data.csv

awk '{ if ($1 > 100) print "High", $1; else print "Low", $1 }' data.txt

docs.title: 05 - Text processing with awk
docs.text:
• awk
  • sed
  • grep

docs.title: Debugging a Java stream in IntelliJ
docs.text:
I just discovered a useful debugging feature that might help you one day!

In IntelliJ, you can debug a stream by setting a breakpoint on it and then clicking ‘Trace Current Stream Chain’ to visualize the flow.

For example, let’s say we have the following Java code:

import java.util.stream.Stream;

public class Main {

    public static void main(String[] args) {
        Stream.of(
                        "camelCase",
                        "PascalCase",
                        "snake_case",
                        "",
                        "kebab-case",
                        "UPPERCASE"
                )
                .filter(s -> !s.isBlank())
                .map(String::toUpperCase)
                .forEach(System.out::println);
    }
}

By clicking ‘Trace Current Stream Chain’

We can have a split mode of each step

Or we can have a global flat mode

docs.title: JSON processing in command line with jq
docs.text: jq is a command line JSON processor which will help you pretty-print and filter a JSON in a terminal

docs.title: Format JSON
docs.text: echo '{"user": {"name": "Elie", "age": 29}}' | jq .

docs.title: Extract specific fields
docs.text: echo '{"user": {"name": "Elie", "age": 29}}' | jq '.user.name'

docs.title: Extract fields from an array
docs.text: echo '[{"project": "frontend", "status": "ok"}, {"project": "backend", "status": "ko"}]' | jq '.[] | .project'

docs.title: Filter an array
docs.text: echo '[{"project": "frontend", "status": "ok"}, {"project": "backend", "status": "ko"}]' | jq '.[] | select(.status=="ok")'

docs.title: Transform JSON
docs.text:
echo '{"firstName": "Elie", "lastName": "Daher"}' | jq '{fullName: (.firstName + " " + .lastName)}'

jq helps specially when we call an endpoint or a cli that returns a JSON response.

docs.title: Deployments
docs.text:
kubectl get deployments -n <namespace>

You can hide headers with --no-headers

kubectl get deployments 
    --no-headers
    -n <namespace>

You can select custom columns, for example to show deployments with available replicas

kubectl get deployments
  -o custom-columns="NAME:.metadata.name, REPLICAS:.status.availableReplicas"
  -n <namespace>

docs.title: Cron Jobs
docs.text:
kubectl get cronjobs -n <namespace>

You can check the last scheduled time, last successful time and suspended info for all cron jobs with : 

kubectl get cronjobs 
   -o custom-columns="NAME:.metadata.name, LAST_SCHEDULED:.status.lastScheduleTime, LAST_SUCCESSFUL=.status.lastSuccessfulTime, ON_HOLD:.spec.suspend"
   -n <namespace>

docs.title: Jobs
docs.text:
kubectl get jobs -n <namespace>

You can get the latest jobs sorted by creation timestamp for a given cron job (by filtering by label)

kubectl get jobs 
    -l app.kubernetes.io/component=<cron-job-name>
    --sort-by=.metadata.creationTimestamp
    -n <namespace>

To return the latest job status for a given cron job, you can run the following command 

kubectl get jobs 
    -l app.kubernetes.io/component=<cron-job-name>
    --sort-by=.metadata.creationTimestamp
    -o jsonpath='{.items[-1].status.conditions[-1].type}'
    -n <namespace>

docs.title: Pods
docs.text:
kubectl get pods -n <namespace>

You can get logs of a given pod 

kubectl get logs <pod-name> -n <namespace>

Tip : You can save terminal output with command > file.txt, for example 

kubectl get logs <pod-name> -n <namespace> > pod-logs.txt

Or you can even append it into an existing file with command >> existing-file.txt, for example 

kubectl get logs <pod-name> -n <namespace> >> pod-logs.txt

You can get more details about the pods with the following command : 

kubectl describe pod <pod-name> -n <namespace>
